label: Dev Terminal
description: Developer-facing SaaS variant with API-first framing and technical product proof.
signatureMotif: terminal previews, code framing, and dense technical product proof
promptHints: Market the product through developer trust, API clarity, and technical proof instead of broad lifestyle benefits.
Treat code examples and product surface previews as first-class design elements.

layouts: Use a left-aligned hero with terse API value proposition, dual CTAs, and a large right-side terminal window showing real request and response flow.
Build feature sections as alternating 60/40 splits where one side shows SDK code tabs and the other side stacks technical proof bullets.
Present integration steps as a horizontal three-step strip of command line, config snippet, and live dashboard result cards.
Use a dense social-proof band made of logo row, uptime or throughput stats, and small open-source or compliance badges in one compressed block.
Structure pricing or plan comparison as dark bordered cards beneath an implementation FAQ, with API limits and developer features surfaced before marketing copy.
motifs: Dark graphite surfaces with thin cool borders, softened 0.85rem corners, and luminous green primary accents that feel like terminal success states.
Terminal chrome, code fences, and monospace labels are first-class framing devices, not decorative snippets buried deep in the layout.
Technical proof appears immediately through response payloads, SDK tabs, metric callouts, and compact badges for uptime, latency, or security claims.
Cards feel layered but restrained, using subtle inner highlights and low-elevation shadows instead of glossy glassmorphism or playful depth tricks.
antiPatterns: Avoid bright startup gradients, abstract blob backgrounds, or pastel illustrations that weaken the developer-tool credibility of the interface.
Never lead with lifestyle photography or team-happy marketing scenes; the hero should prove the product through code and system behavior.
Avoid oversized copy-only sections with vague platform promises when a terminal preview, API example, or benchmark could carry the message.
Do not use soft consumer-app pricing tables with cute icons; plans should read like infrastructure choices with limits, features, and implementation detail.
